network.overall_strategy: This network treats prebiotic arginine synthesis as unresolved and therefore centers literature-supported precursor-generating chemistry rather than overstating a solved route. The dominant constructive branch follows the cyanosulfidic protometabolism sequence of Patel et al. (2015) and Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland (2017): HCN and acetylene are coupled under Cu photoredox conditions to acrylonitrile, converted to beta-aminopropionitrile, reduced to beta-aminopropionaldehyde, guanylated by cyanamide, and advanced to an arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor that hydrolyzes to arginine. Parallel lower-confidence but literature-discussed branches include direct ornithine guanylation by cyanamide, a hypothetical cyanate/citrulline prebiotic urea-cycle analog, and a trace supercritical CO2/H2O branch from pyruvic acid plus hydroxylamine. Hydrothermal chemistry is used conservatively to supply reduced carbon feedstocks such as formate and pyruvate, while surface photochemistry performs the key C-N bond-forming and guanidinium-installing steps. Biochemical/prebiotic stages emphasize hydrolysis, concentration, montmorillonite capture, and peptide incorporation rather than claiming de novo high-yield synthesis under conditions known to destroy arginine.

reactions.name: Greigite-assisted hydrothermal reduction of CO2 to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, anoxic, H2-rich, pH 9-11

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface electron transfer reduces CO2 with H2 to one-carbon reduced carbon species, represented here as formate.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction on Fe-S minerals is well supported as a source of reduced carbon feedstocks in alkaline vent settings; this network uses it conservatively as upstream provisioning rather than a direct arginine-forming step (Huber & Wachtershauser, 1997; Jordan et al., 2021; Kitadai, 2015).

reactions.name: Magnetite/greigite-mediated hydrothermal formation of pyruvate
reactions.mechanism: Mineral-catalyzed multicarbon fixation from CO2/H2 yields alpha-keto acid products represented by pyruvate.
reactions.reasoning: Pyruvate is included as a hydrothermal carbon-fixation product because the user instructions require such network interconnection, but this branch is kept separate from the main arginine route because arginine is thermally labile in hot hydrothermal settings and the literature does not establish a continuous pyruvate-to-arginine sequence (Amend & Shock, 1998; Kitadai, 2015; Miller & Bada, 1988; Korner et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Cu-photoredox cyanosulfidic coupling of HCN and acetylene to acrylonitrile
agents: sulfide minerals (Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system on sulfide mineral surfaces)

conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, H2S present, UV irradiation in shallow surface pool

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox Cu(I)-mediated cross-coupling of HCN and acetylene under cyanosulfidic conditions yields acrylonitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the first explicit step of the literature-supported cyanosulfidic arginine-precursor route and must retain the reported environment: HCN, H2S, Cu photoredox, phosphate buffer, UV, and 35C anaerobic conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Amination of acrylonitrile to beta-aminopropionitrile

conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, H2S present, UV-assisted surface chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Conjugate addition of ammonia to activated nitrile alkene gives beta-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: Beta-aminopropionitrile is the documented next intermediate in the Patel/Sutherland arginine-precursor branch. Conditions are matched to the cyanosulfidic network rather than generalized (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photoreductive conversion of beta-aminopropionitrile to beta-aminopropionaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: Cu photoredox with UV irradiation; TiO2 as photocatalytic mineral surface
conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, H2S present, photoreduction in shallow pool

reactions.mechanism: Photoredox reduction of nitrile-derived intermediate furnishes the corresponding amino aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. describe reduction to beta-aminopropionaldehyde within the cyanosulfidic network. TiO2 is included as a plausible mineral photocatalytic surface for the surface-environment requirement, while the reaction rationale remains anchored in the Cu/H2S/UV system of the reported pathway (Patel et al., 2015; Negron-Mendoza et al., 2012; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Photochemical generation of cyanamide from reduced atmospheric precursors
agents: sulfide minerals (photochemically active reduced sulfur context)

conditions.other: anoxic atmosphere/pool interface; UV photochemistry; included as an environmental source term rather than a quantitatively closed mechanistic laboratory sequence

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reduced-atmosphere chemistry generates cyanamide as a prebiotic guanylating/condensing agent.
reactions.reasoning: The literature summary explicitly supports cyanamide as prebiotically available from UV irradiation of reduced atmospheric mixtures, but not as a single definitive aqueous stoichiometric step from HCN plus NH3 alone. This node is therefore intentionally framed as an environmental photochemical source consistent with the evidence, not an overclaimed detailed mechanism (Schimpl, Lemmon & Calvin, 1965; Patel et al., 2015; Powner, Gerland & Sutherland, 2009).

reactions.name: UV ammonium-cyanide chemistry to guanidine

conditions.other: pH 8.4-8.5, 254 nm UV, 7 days to 10 weeks, anoxic ammonium cyanide solution

reactions.mechanism: UV irradiation of ammonium cyanide solutions yields guanidine among stable nitrogenous products.
reactions.reasoning: Lohrmann directly reported guanidine production in 8-20% yield from irradiated ammonium cyanide solutions under mildly basic conditions with iron catalyst context; pyrite is used here as the required mineral-surface iron source consistent with the broader iron-rich prebiotic surface setting (Lohrmann, 1972; Robertson & Miller, 1995).

reactions.name: Guanylation of beta-aminopropionaldehyde by cyanamide toward arginine aminonitrile precursor

conditions.other: 35C, anaerobic, H2S present, UV; captures guanylation plus further photoreduction/HCN addition compressed to the reported alpha-aminonitrile branch point

reactions.mechanism: Primary amine attack on cyanamide installs the guanidino functionality; subsequent cyanosulfidic photoreduction/HCN addition yields the arginine alpha-aminonitrile precursor represented as compound 46-like product.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. explicitly state that the especially efficient reaction of beta-aminopropionaldehyde with cyanamide suggests production of compound 46, the alpha-aminonitrile precursor of arginine, alongside the proline branch. Because the paper does not provide a separately isolated continuous stoichiometric sequence for every substep, this node intentionally follows the literature's reported branch point without inventing unsupported intermediates (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of arginine aminonitrile precursor to arginine

conditions.other: aqueous hydrolysis during pool aging or geothermal cycling

reactions.mechanism: Nitrile hydrolysis converts the alpha-aminonitrile precursor to the corresponding alpha-amino acid arginine.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of alpha-aminonitriles to amino acids is the expected terminal step of Strecker-like and cyanosulfidic amino acid precursor chemistry; here it completes the Patel/Tsanakopoulou arginine branch without claiming reported high yield for arginine itself, which the literature explicitly says remains undemonstrated as a full continuous experiment (Patel et al., 2015; Tsanakopoulou & Sutherland, 2017; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Direct guanylation of ornithine by cyanamide to arginine

conditions.catalyst: mineral-concentrating surface; basic to near-neutral aqueous conditions
conditions.other: concept anchored in classic Ba(OH)2 guanylation and modern aqueous cyanamide guanylation; represented here as a prebiotic concentration-assisted surface analogue, not a fully demonstrated prebiotic high-yield experiment

reactions.mechanism: The terminal primary amine of ornithine attacks the electrophilic carbon of cyanamide to form the guanidinium side chain of arginine.
reactions.reasoning: This is the classic two-phase strategy emphasized in the literature: preform the C5 diamino backbone as ornithine, then install the guanidinium group with cyanamide. The chemistry was first demonstrated by Schulze & Winterstein and later shown to proceed efficiently in water under non-prebiotic catalysis; the present node is therefore chemically well founded but explicitly lower-confidence as a strictly prebiotic route (Schulze & Winterstein, 1899; Tsubokura et al., 2014; Johnson et al., 2008; Meierhenrich et al., 2004).

reactions.name: Carbamoylation of ornithine by cyanate to citrulline

conditions.other: hypothetical prebiotic urea-cycle analog under anoxic surface conditions

reactions.mechanism: Cyanate carbamoylates the terminal amino group of ornithine to give citrulline.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically identifies cyanate as a plausible prebiotic carbamoyl donor more stable than carbamoyl phosphate and presents ornithine to citrulline as a reasonable prebiotic urea-cycle analog. This step is included as an explicitly hypothetical branch, not as an experimentally demonstrated arginine route (Juarez et al., 2021; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Speculative conversion of citrulline and cyanamide to arginine

conditions.other: exploratory prebiotic pool chemistry; not experimentally established

reactions.mechanism: Hypothetical guanyl-group reorganization/addition from cyanamide in a citrulline-containing network gives arginine.
reactions.reasoning: The research report is clear that a prebiotic urea-cycle analog remains entirely hypothetical and that conversion from citrulline toward arginine lacks direct experimental demonstration. This node is retained only to encode the literature-discussed possibility and is explicitly speculative (Ferris et al., 1978; Juarez et al., 2021; Miller & Orgel, 1974).

reactions.name: Trace supercritical CO2/H2O production of arginine from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine

conditions.catalyst: supercritical CO2/H2O medium; mineral surfaces included as hydrothermal context
conditions.other: 60C, supercritical CO2/H2O, trace yield after acid hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Unresolved multistep condensative/reductive chemistry from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine produces trace arginine detectable after hydrolysis.
reactions.reasoning: Fujioka et al. reported only 0.32 micromol arginine under supercritical CO2/H2O conditions from hydroxylamine hydrochloride and pyruvic acid, with no mechanism and vastly greater alanine production. This branch is therefore included strictly as a marginal trace route and not treated as a core productive pathway, in line with the literature's caution and with arginine's hydrothermal lability (Fujioka et al., 2009; Miller & Bada, 1988; Korner et al., 2021).

reactions.name: Montmorillonite adsorption and concentration of arginine
reactions.mechanism: Cationic guanidinium-bearing arginine partitions into montmorillonite interlayers by cation exchange, increasing local concentration and persistence.
reactions.reasoning: This is a non-stoichiometric concentration/preservation node included because recent work shows montmorillonite selectively adsorbs arginine 30-50x more strongly than neutral amino acids, plausibly protecting it from decomposition and UV damage once formed (Catalano et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Wet-dry peptide bond formation from arginine to arginylarginine

conditions.other: wet-dry cycling on clay/mineral surfaces; concentrated amino acid film

reactions.mechanism: Dehydrative condensation during geothermal wet-dry cycling forms a dipeptide from concentrated arginine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports amino-acid oligomerization during wet-dry cycles and specifically notes montmorillonite-arginine complexes can undergo peptide bond formation under dry high-temperature conditions. This node represents downstream biochemical incorporation, not evidence for easier de novo arginine synthesis (Campbell et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2019; Huber & Wachtershauser, 1998).

pathways.name: Canonical cyanosulfidic arginine precursor route
pathways.description: Best-supported constructive route from the literature: HCN/acetylene coupling, amination, photoreduction, cyanamide guanylation, and hydrolysis to arginine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic route with independent guanidine-rich nitrogen chemistry context
pathways.description: Same core Patel/Sutherland branch, but occurring in a pool where UV ammonium-cyanide chemistry also supplies guanidine as a parallel nitrogen-rich product.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon provisioning into cyanosulfidic arginine synthesis
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction provides reduced carbon feedstock context that feeds surface HCN-rich chemistry, which then follows the literature-supported cyanosulfidic route.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine cyanamide guanylation
pathways.description: Conceptually simplest literature-backed route: once ornithine and cyanamide co-occur at the surface, guanylation gives arginine directly.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation with clay concentration/protection
pathways.description: Direct ornithine-to-arginine conversion followed by montmorillonite capture to stabilize and concentrate the product.

pathways.name: Direct ornithine guanylation followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: Arginine formed by ornithine guanylation is subsequently incorporated into a dipeptide under wet-dry biochemical cycling.

pathways.name: Hypothetical cyanate-citrulline prebiotic urea-cycle analog
pathways.description: Exploratory branch discussed in the literature: ornithine carbamoylation by cyanate to citrulline, then speculative advancement to arginine in cyanamide-containing pools.

pathways.name: Cyanate-citrulline branch with independent cyanamide source
pathways.description: Same hypothetical urea-cycle analog, explicitly coupled to a photochemical cyanamide source on the surface.

pathways.name: Trace supercritical pyruvate-hydroxylamine route
pathways.description: Marginal literature-reported trace formation of arginine under supercritical CO2/H2O from pyruvic acid and hydroxylamine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic arginine route followed by clay capture and peptide incorporation
pathways.description: The strongest precursor-forming branch is extended into plausible postsynthetic persistence and utilization: hydrolysis to arginine, adsorption on montmorillonite, then wet-dry peptide formation.
